Radial symmetry describes the direction and equal-magnitude behavior of the electric field.
The calculation is an application of electric flux through a closed surface.
E can be pulled out of the surface integral only when symmetry makes its magnitude constant on the spherical surface.
The enclosed charge is obtained by multiplying uniform volume charge density by enclosed volume.
The field is derived by applying Gauss law to an inside spherical Gaussian surface.
The left side of Gauss law becomes E 4*pi*r^2.
The enclosed charge for r < R is Q r^3/R^3, not Q.
The outside field follows from Gauss law with total charge enclosed.
The Gaussian surface flux is E 4*pi*r^2.
Potential values require a chosen reference level.
Potential difference is defined through work per unit charge against the electric field.
The integral uses the electric field along the path.
Continuity is used when integrating piecewise electric fields across regions.
Region selection is needed before evaluating a piecewise potential expression.
Outside a spherical charge distribution has the same potential as a point charge Q at the center.
The outside potential follows by integrating the outside field with V(infinity)=0.
The constant is chosen using V(infinity)=0.
The inside potential is obtained by integrating the inside field E = kQr/R^3.
The boundary value V(R) comes from the outside potential expression.
The inside expression follows from V(r)-V(R) = - integral_R^r E_inside dr.
The integration constant is fixed by matching the inside potential to the outside value at r = R.
The quadratic expression is valid only for points inside the solid sphere.
The result follows from zero electric field inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium.
The surface potential is the outside value at r = R.
The insulating solid sphere has volume charge and quadratic inside potential.
The conducting sphere has zero interior field and constant inside potential.
